- `AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T` (definition) — the Nagata change-of-variables algebra

- `AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1` (definition) — the single-variable companion shift

- `AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg` (lemma) — `T1 c ∘ T1 (-c) = id`, i.e. the

- `AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up` (lemma) — a bookkeeping bound: if every exponent

- `AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne` (lemma) — distinct exponent vectors `v ≠ w`

120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."

136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149

151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. 160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.

205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)

222	\end{lemma}

224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

8	/-!
9	# Generic flatness of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)
10	
11	This file packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4 **generic flatness**
12	theorem: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent `𝓞_X`-module on a
13	finite-type morphism `X ⟶ S` becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open
14	subscheme of `S`. This is the inductive engine underlying the
15	flattening-stratification construction of Grothendieck's existence theorem for
16	the Quot scheme.
17	
18	Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the *intended* substantive type
19	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
20	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a `sorry`
21	body where the proof is not yet supplied. The substantive generic-flatness
22	proof is deep (Nitsure~\S4: prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
23	clearing denominators).
24	
25	The blueprint-pinned declaration is:
26	
27	- `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem) — **generic flatness**
28	  [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf
29	  on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. Its
30	  algebraic form (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin) is the
31	  statement that a finite module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian
32	  domain becomes free after inverting one non-zero element of the base.
33	
34	The file-internal `GenericFreeness` namespace lands the **finite-module /
35	finite-morphism special case** of the algebraic form axiom-clean, as a reusable
36	building block for the dévissage's leaves.
37	
38	## Note on type expressivity
39	
40	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
41	`genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V` and
42	substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers are necessary
43	(without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
44	
45	## Mathlib status
46	
47	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides `Module.Flat`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat`
48	(morphism-level), `IsImmersion`, `IsLocallyNoetherian`, `IsIntegral`,
49	`LocallyOfFiniteType`, and the dévissage / Noether-normalisation infrastructure
50	the algebraic generic-flatness argument consumes. It does NOT yet provide the
51	polynomial-ring core of generic freeness, nor the geometric `genericFlatness`
52	statement packaging it over a noetherian integral base.
53	
54	## References
55	
56	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`.
57	Source: Nitsure, "Construction of Hilbert and Quot schemes", §4 (FGA Explained
58	Ch. 5, arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–18); Stacks Project tag 00HB (module flat).

69	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — algebraic generic freeness (finite case)
70	
71	This section builds the **module-theoretic** generic-freeness statements that

74	
75	The full algebraic statement — `A` a noetherian domain, `B` a *finite-type*
76	`A`-algebra, `M` a finite `B`-module ⟹ `∃ f ≠ 0` with `M_f` free over `A_f` —
77	is a deep theorem (prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
78	clearing denominators). Mathlib already supplies most of the dévissage
79	machinery (`IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime`,
80	`ModuleCat.free_shortExact`, `exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg`), but it does
81	**not** yet contain the polynomial-ring core (generic freeness for a finite
82	module over `A[X₁,…,X_d]`). See the file `task_results` handoff for the
83	precise decomposition of the remaining gap.
84	
85	What we *can* land axiom-clean here is the **finite-module / finite-morphism
86	case**: when `M` is finite as an `A`-module (in particular when `B` is
87	module-finite over `A`), generic freeness follows directly from
88	`Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` applied at the
89	generic point `Frac A`, where `M ⊗_A Frac A` is a finite vector space hence
90	free. This is a genuine special case of the algebraic generic-freeness
91	theorem (the case of a *finite* morphism `X → S`), and a reusable building
92	block for the dévissage's leaves. -/

96	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-module case.** For a noetherian integral domain
97	`A` and a finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that the
98	localisation `M_f` is free over `A_f = Localization.Away f`.
99	
100	This is the `d = 0` (finite-morphism) special case of the algebraic
101	generic-flatness theorem (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4):
102	inverting the generic point `Frac A`, the localised module is a finite vector
103	space hence free, and `Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers`
104	descends that freeness to a single basic open `D(f) ⊆ Spec A`. -/
105	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite
106	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
107	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
108	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
109	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
110	  haveI : Module.FinitePresentation A M := Module.finitePresentation_of_finite A M
111	  obtain ⟨r, hr, hfree, _⟩ :=
112	    Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers (nonZeroDivisors A)
113	      (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap (nonZeroDivisors A) M) (FractionRing A)
114	  exact ⟨r, nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero hr, hfree⟩
115	
116	/-- **Generic flatness, finite-module case.** The flatness form of
117	`exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite`: for a noetherian domain `A` and a
118	finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f` with `M_f` flat over `A_f`.
119	This is the affine-local content of `genericFlatness` for a finite morphism. -/
120	theorem exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite
121	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
122	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
123	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
124	      Module.Flat (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
125	  obtain ⟨f, hf, hfree⟩ := exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M
126	  haveI := hfree
127	  exact ⟨f, hf, Module.Flat.of_free⟩
128	
129	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-morphism case.** If `A` is a noetherian domain,
130	`B` a *module-finite* `A`-algebra, and `M` a finite `B`-module (with the
131	compatible `A`-module structure), then there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` with `M_f`
132	free over `A_f`. Reduces to `exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite` via
133	`Module.Finite.trans` (a finite module over a module-finite algebra is finite
134	over the base). This is generic flatness for a *finite* morphism `X → S`. -/
135	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite
136	    (A : Type*) (B : Type*) (M : Type*)
137	    [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
138	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Module.Finite A B]
139	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
140	    [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
141	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
142	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
143	  haveI : Module.Finite A M := Module.Finite.trans B M
144	  exact exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M

146	/-! ### Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue (Nitsure §4)
147	
148	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
149	`genericFlatnessAlgebraic` — the case where `M` is finite over the finite-type
150	`A`-algebra `B` but not module-finite over `A` — into the classical Nitsure §4
151	induction. They are ordered so each step uses only earlier blocks (or Mathlib
152	anchors). Throughout, `A` is a noetherian domain with fraction field
153	`K = Frac A`, `B` a finite-type `A`-algebra, and `M` a finite `B`-module with
154	the compatible `A`-module structure via the scalar tower `A → B → M`. -/
155	
156	/-- **L1 — torsion base case `M_K = 0`** (`lem:gf_torsion_base`, Nitsure §4 base
157	case of the induction). If the generic localisation `M_K = K ⊗_A M` (encoded as
158	`LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors A) M`) is trivial and `M` is a finite
159	`B`-module, then there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` with `f • M = 0`; consequently
160	`M_f = 0` is free over `A_f`.
161	
162	The hypothesis `Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors A) M)` is the
163	formal encoding of `M_K = 0`: localising `M` at the non-zero divisors of the
164	domain `A` inverts exactly the non-zero elements, so `M_K = S^{-1}M` with
165	`S = A \ {0}`. Each generator of the finite `B`-module `M` is then killed by a
166	non-zero element of `A`; the product `f` of those annihilators (non-zero since
167	`A` is a domain) annihilates all of `M`, whence `M_f` is subsingleton and free. -/
168	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion
169	    (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A]
170	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B]
171	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
172	    [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M]
173	    (htors : Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors A) M)) :
174	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
175	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
176	  -- The `A`/`B` scalar actions commute (A acts through `algebraMap A B`).
177	  have hcomm : ∀ (a : A) (b : B) (x : M), a • b • x = b • a • x := fun a b x => by
178	    rw [← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul B a (b • x), ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul B a x,
179	      smul_smul, smul_smul, mul_comm]
180	  -- Torsion: every element of `M` is killed by a non-zero divisor of `A`.
181	  rw [LocalizedModule.subsingleton_iff] at htors
182	  choose r hr using htors
183	  -- `r m ∈ A⁰` and `(r m) • m = 0`.
184	  -- A finite `B`-generating set of `M`.
185	  obtain ⟨S, hS⟩ := (Module.Finite.fg_top : (⊤ : Submodule B M).FG)
186	  -- The product of the chosen annihilators of the generators.
187	  set f : A := ∏ x ∈ S, r x with hf_def
188	  have hfmem : f ∈ nonZeroDivisors A := prod_mem fun x _ => (hr x).1
189	  have hf0 : f ≠ 0 := nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero hfmem
190	  -- `f` annihilates every generator, hence all of `span B S = ⊤ = M`.
191	  have hgen : ∀ x ∈ S, f • x = (0 : M) := by
192	    intro x hx
193	    classical
194	    rw [hf_def, ← Finset.prod_erase_mul S r hx, mul_smul, (hr x).2, smul_zero]
195	  have hfM : ∀ m : M, f • m = (0 : M) := by
196	    intro m
197	    have hmem : m ∈ Submodule.span B (↑S : Set M) := by rw [hS]; exact Submodule.mem_top
198	    induction hmem using Submodule.span_induction with
199	    | mem x hx => exact hgen x hx
200	    | zero => exact smul_zero f
201	    | add x y _ _ ihx ihy => rw [smul_add, ihx, ihy, add_zero]
202	    | smul b x _ ih => rw [hcomm, ih, smul_zero]
203	  -- `f • M = 0` ⟹ `M_f` subsingleton ⟹ free over `A_f`.
204	  refine ⟨f, hf0, ?_⟩
205	  haveI : Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) :=
206	    LocalizedModule.subsingleton_iff.mpr fun m => ⟨f, Submonoid.mem_powers f, hfM m⟩
207	  exact Module.Free.of_subsingleton _ _

209	/-- **L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact**
210	(`lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact`, Nitsure §4). Given a short exact
211	sequence `0 → M' → M → M'' → 0` of `B`-modules and `f ∈ A`, restricting scalars
212	along `A → B` and localising every term at the powers of `f` yields a short
213	exact sequence `0 → M'_f → M_f → M''_f → 0` of `A_f`-modules; the localised
214	`i_f` is injective and the localised `q_f` is surjective.
215	
216	Localisation of modules at a multiplicative set is exact, so it preserves the
217	injection `i`, the surjection `q`, and exactness at the middle term
218	(`LocalizedModule.map_injective` / `…map_surjective` / `…map_exact` applied to
219	the scalar-restricted maps). -/
220	theorem exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact
221	    (A B M' M M'' : Type*) [CommRing A]
222	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B]
223	    [AddCommGroup M'] [Module B M'] [Module A M'] [IsScalarTower A B M']
224	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M]
225	    [AddCommGroup M''] [Module B M''] [Module A M''] [IsScalarTower A B M'']
226	    (i : M' →ₗ[B] M) (q : M →ₗ[B] M'')
227	    (hi : Function.Injective i) (hq : Function.Surjective q)
228	    (hexact : Function.Exact i q)
229	    (f : A) :
230	    Function.Injective (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (i.restrictScalars A)) ∧
231	    Function.Surjective (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (q.restrictScalars A)) ∧
232	    Function.Exact (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (i.restrictScalars A))
233	      (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (q.restrictScalars A)) := by
234	  refine ⟨LocalizedModule.map_injective _ (i.restrictScalars A) hi,
235	    LocalizedModule.map_surjective _ (q.restrictScalars A) hq, ?_⟩
236	  exact LocalizedModule.map_exact (Submonoid.powers f) (i.restrictScalars A)
237	    (q.restrictScalars A) hexact

240	(`lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport`, Nitsure §4). Let `N` be an
241	`A`-module and `f', f'' ∈ A` with `f = f' f''`. If `N_{f'}` is free over
242	`A_{f'}`, then `N_f` is free over `A_f`.

245	image of `f''` (`IsLocalization.Away.awayToAwayLeft` / `IsLocalization.Away.mul'`),
246	and `N_f` is the corresponding localisation of `N_{f'}`; a localisation of a free
247	module is free over the localised base ring (`Module.free_of_isLocalizedModule`). -/
248	theorem free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul
249	    (A N : Type*) [CommRing A] [AddCommGroup N] [Module A N]
250	    {f f' f'' : A} (hf : f = f' * f'')
251	    (hN' : Module.Free (Localization.Away f')
252	      (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f') N)) :
253	    Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
254	      (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N) := by
255	  subst hf
256	  haveI : Module.Free (Localization.Away f')
257	    (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f') N) := hN'
258	  -- `A_f` also localises `f'' * f'` (= `f' * f''`).
259	  haveI hloc : IsLocalization.Away (f'' * f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')) := by
260	    rw [mul_comm]; infer_instance
261	  -- The ring map `A_{f'} → A_{f'f''}` and the `A`-scalar-tower it sits in.
262	  letI algA' : Algebra (Localization.Away f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')) :=
263	    (IsLocalization.Away.awayToAwayLeft (R := A) (S := Localization.Away f')
264	      (P := Localization.Away (f' * f'')) f' f'').toAlgebra

266	    IsScalarTower.of_algebraMap_eq fun a =>
267	      (IsLocalization.Away.awayToAwayLeft_eq (S := Localization.Away f') f' f'' a).symm
268	  -- Restrict scalars along `A_{f'} → A_{f'f''}` to make `N_f` an `A_{f'}`-module.
269	  letI modA' : Module (Localization.Away f') (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N) :=
270	    Module.compHom _ (algebraMap (Localization.Away f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')))
271	  haveI towerA'Af : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away f') (Localization.Away (f' * f''))
272	      (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N) :=
273	    IsScalarTower.of_algebraMap_smul fun _ _ => rfl
274	  haveI towerAA' : IsScalarTower A (Localization.Away f')
275	      (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N) := by
276	    refine ⟨fun a a' x => ?_⟩
277	    have hcompA' : ∀ (b : Localization.Away f')
278	        (y : LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N),
279	        b • y = algebraMap (Localization.Away f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')) b • y :=

282	      ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_apply A (Localization.Away f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')) a,
283	      mul_smul, IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul]

285	  have hunit : ∀ x : Submonoid.powers f',

289	        (Module.End A (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N)) (f' * f'')) :=
290	      IsLocalizedModule.map_units

292	        ⟨f' * f'', Submonoid.mem_powers _⟩

294	    have hcomm : Commute
295	        (algebraMap A (Module.End A (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N)) f')
296	        (algebraMap A (Module.End A (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N)) f'') :=
297	      (Commute.all _ _).map _

299	    rintro ⟨x, n, rfl⟩

302	  let φ : LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f') N →ₗ[A]

304	    IsLocalizedModule.lift (Submonoid.powers f') (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap _ N)
305	      (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap _ N) hunit

308	    LinearMap.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization (Submonoid.powers f') (Localization.Away f') φ

313	    IsLocalizedModule.isBaseChange (Submonoid.powers f') (Localization.Away f') _

333	(`Module.projective_lifting_property`); the splitting
334	(`Function.Exact.splitSurjectiveEquiv`) gives `Q ≃ₗ[R] P × T`, and `P × T` is
335	free, hence so is `Q` (`Module.Free.of_equiv`). -/
336	theorem free_of_shortExact_of_free_free
337	    (R P Q T : Type*) [CommRing R]
338	    [AddCommGroup P] [Module R P] [Module.Free R P]
339	    [AddCommGroup Q] [Module R Q]
340	    [AddCommGroup T] [Module R T] [Module.Free R T]
341	    (iota : P →ₗ[R] Q) (pi : Q →ₗ[R] T)
342	    (hiota : Function.Injective iota) (hpi : Function.Surjective pi)
343	    (hexact : Function.Exact iota pi) :
344	    Module.Free R Q := by
345	  obtain ⟨l, hl⟩ := Module.projective_lifting_property pi LinearMap.id hpi
346	  obtain ⟨e, -⟩ := hexact.splitSurjectiveEquiv hiota ⟨l, hl⟩
347	  exact Module.Free.of_equiv e.symm

361	    (A B M' M M'' : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A]
362	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B]

364	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M]
365	    [AddCommGroup M''] [Module B M''] [Module A M''] [IsScalarTower A B M'']
366	    (i : M' →ₗ[B] M) (q : M →ₗ[B] M'')

368	    (hexact : Function.Exact i q)

397	Nitsure §4 common-denominator step). For `A` a noetherian domain with fraction
398	field `K = Frac A` and a polynomial `p ∈ K[X_1,…,X_n]`, there is a non-zero
399	`g ∈ A` such that `p` lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
400	`A_g[X_1,…,X_n] → K[X_1,…,X_n]`, where `A_g = Localization.Away g`; equivalently
401	every coefficient of `p` already lies in the subring `A_g ⊆ K`.
402	
403	The denominator-clearing engine: take `g` to be a common denominator of the
404	finitely many coefficients of `p` (Mathlib's
405	`IsLocalization.exist_integer_multiples` over the support), so each coefficient
406	`c = a / g ∈ A_g`. The witness polynomial is the numerator polynomial scaled by
407	the unit `g⁻¹ ∈ A_g`. This is the shared primitive the Noether-normalisation L4
408	denominator-clear and the torsion-reindex (L5b) both consume. -/
409	theorem gf_clear_one_denominator
410	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
411	    (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
412	    ∃ (g : A) (hg : g ≠ 0),
413	      p ∈ Set.range (MvPolynomial.map
414	        (IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A)
415	          (show Submonoid.powers g ≤ Submonoid.comap (RingHom.id A) (nonZeroDivisors A) by
416	            rw [Submonoid.powers_le]
417	            simpa using mem_nonZeroDivisors_of_ne_zero hg)
418	          : Localization.Away g →+* FractionRing A)) := by
419	  classical
420	  obtain ⟨s, hs⟩ := IsLocalization.exist_integer_multiples (nonZeroDivisors A)
421	    p.support (fun i => MvPolynomial.coeff i p)
422	  refine ⟨s, nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero s.2, ?_⟩
423	  have hle : Submonoid.powers (↑s : A) ≤ Submonoid.comap (RingHom.id A) (nonZeroDivisors A) := by
424	    rw [Submonoid.powers_le]; exact s.2
425	  set φ : Localization.Away (↑s : A) →+* FractionRing A :=
426	    IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle with hφ
427	  have hunit : IsUnit (algebraMap A (Localization.Away (↑s : A)) ↑s) :=
428	    IsLocalization.map_units (Localization.Away (↑s : A))
429	      (⟨↑s, Submonoid.mem_powers _⟩ : Submonoid.powers (↑s : A))
430	  have hnum : ∀ i, ∃ a : A, (algebraMap A (FractionRing A)) a =
431	      (algebraMap A (FractionRing A) ↑s) * MvPolynomial.coeff i p := by
432	    intro i
433	    by_cases hi : i ∈ p.support
434	    · obtain ⟨a, ha⟩ := hs i hi
435	      exact ⟨a, by rw [ha, Algebra.smul_def]⟩
436	    · exact ⟨0, by rw [MvPolynomial.notMem_support_iff.mp hi]; simp⟩
437	  choose a ha using hnum
438	  have hφs : φ (algebraMap A (Localization.Away (↑s:A)) ↑s)
439	      = algebraMap A (FractionRing A) ↑s := by rw [hφ, IsLocalization.map_eq]; rfl
440	  have hee : (↑(hunit.unit⁻¹) : Localization.Away (↑s:A))
441	      * algebraMap A (Localization.Away (↑s:A)) ↑s = 1 := by
442	    have h := Units.inv_mul hunit.unit
443	    rwa [hunit.unit_spec] at h
444	  have hφinv : φ (↑(hunit.unit⁻¹) : Localization.Away (↑s:A))
445	      * algebraMap A (FractionRing A) ↑s = 1 := by
446	    rw [← hφs, ← map_mul, hee, map_one]
447	  -- The preimage polynomial: the numerator polynomial scaled by the unit `g⁻¹`.
448	  refine ⟨MvPolynomial.C (↑(hunit.unit⁻¹) : Localization.Away (↑s:A))
449	        * (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away (↑s:A)))
450	            (∑ i ∈ p.support, MvPolynomial.monomial i (a i))), ?_⟩
451	  apply MvPolynomial.ext
452	  intro i
453	  rw [MvPolynomial.coeff_map, MvPolynomial.coeff_C_mul, map_mul, MvPolynomial.coeff_map,
454	    IsLocalization.map_eq]
455	  have hcoeff : MvPolynomial.coeff i (∑ j ∈ p.support, MvPolynomial.monomial j (a j)) = a i := by
456	    rw [MvPolynomial.coeff_sum]
457	    simp only [MvPolynomial.coeff_monomial, Finset.sum_ite_eq']
458	    by_cases hi : i ∈ p.support
459	    · rw [if_pos hi]
460	    · rw [if_neg hi]
461	      have hz : MvPolynomial.coeff i p = 0 := MvPolynomial.notMem_support_iff.mp hi
462	      have h2 := ha i
463	      rw [hz, mul_zero] at h2
464	      exact (IsFractionRing.injective A (FractionRing A) (by rw [h2, map_zero])).symm
465	  rw [hcoeff, RingHom.id_apply, ha i, ← mul_assoc, hφinv, one_mul]

470	`B_K = K ⊗_A B ≠ 0`, there exist `g ∈ A`, `g ≠ 0`, a number `n`, and an
471	injective `A_g`-algebra map `φ : A_g[X_1,…,X_n] → B_g` (the `b_j := φ(X_j)`
472	algebraically independent over `K`) such that `B_g` is module-finite over

518	/-- **L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence** (`lem:gf_generic_rank_ses`,
519	Nitsure §4 inductive step). For `A` a noetherian domain and `d ≥ 0`, a finite
520	module `N` over the polynomial ring `P_d := A[X_1,…,X_d]`, there exist `m ∈ ℕ`
521	and an injective `P_d`-linear map `φ : P_d^{⊕m} → N` whose cokernel
522	`T := N ⧸ range φ` is a torsion `P_d`-module. Equivalently, a short exact
523	sequence `0 → P_d^{⊕m} → N → T → 0` with `T` torsion.
524	
525	`m` is the generic rank: `dim_{Frac P_d} (Frac P_d ⊗_{P_d} N)`. The map `φ`
526	is the `P_d`-linear combination of `m` lifts `v : Fin m → N` whose images form a
527	`Frac P_d`-basis of the localisation `N_Q := LocalizedModule (P_d)⁰ N`. This step
528	is built over `P_d` directly — no inversion of any `g ∈ A` is required. -/
529	theorem gf_generic_rank_ses
530	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
531	    (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
532	    [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
533	    [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
534	    ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
535	        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
536	      Function.Injective φ ∧
537	      Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ) := by
538	  classical
539	  -- Notation: `P = A[X_1,…,X_d]`, its non-zero divisors `S`, fraction field `K`,
540	  -- localisation `NK = N_Q`, and the localisation map `ℓ : N → NK`.
541	  let P := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A
542	  let S := nonZeroDivisors P
543	  let K := Localization S
544	  let NK := LocalizedModule S N
545	  let ℓ : N →ₗ[P] NK := LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap S N
546	  -- `NK` is a finite-dimensional `K`-vector space; take a basis indexed by `Fin m`.
547	  let m := Module.finrank K NK
548	  let b : Module.Basis (Fin m) K NK := Module.finBasis K NK
549	  -- Lift each basis vector along `ℓ`, clearing its denominator.
550	  obtain ⟨lift, hlift⟩ :
551	      ∃ lift : Fin m → N × S, ∀ i, ((lift i).2 : P) • b i = ℓ (lift i).1 := by
552	    refine ⟨fun i => (IsLocalizedModule.surj S ℓ (b i)).choose, fun i => ?_⟩
553	    exact (IsLocalizedModule.surj S ℓ (b i)).choose_spec
554	  let v : Fin m → N := fun i => (lift i).1
555	  -- The denominators map to units of `K`.
556	  let u : Fin m → Kˣ := fun i => (IsLocalization.map_units K (lift i).2).unit
557	  have hlv : ∀ i, ℓ (v i) = (↑(u i) : K) • b i := by
558	    intro i
559	    have hu : (↑(u i) : K) = algebraMap P K ((lift i).2 : P) :=
560	      (IsLocalization.map_units K (lift i).2).unit_spec
561	    rw [hu, algebraMap_smul]
562	    exact (hlift i).symm
563	  -- The images `ℓ (v i)` are linearly independent over `K` (unit multiples of a basis).
564	  have hLIK : LinearIndependent K (fun i => ℓ (v i)) := by
565	    have h := b.linearIndependent.units_smul u
566	    have heq : (fun i => ℓ (v i)) = (u • (⇑b)) := by
567	      funext i; rw [hlv i]; rfl
568	    rw [heq]; exact h
569	  -- Restrict scalars to `P` (the inclusion `P → K` is injective), then descend to `v`.
570	  have hLIP : LinearIndependent P (fun i => ℓ (v i)) := by
571	    refine hLIK.restrict_scalars ?_
572	    intro x y hxy
573	    apply IsFractionRing.injective P K
574	    rw [Algebra.algebraMap_eq_smul_one, Algebra.algebraMap_eq_smul_one]
575	    exact hxy
576	  have hLIv : LinearIndependent P v := LinearIndependent.of_comp ℓ hLIP
577	  -- The map `φ : P^{⊕m} → N`, `e_i ↦ v i`.
578	  refine ⟨m, Fintype.linearCombination P v, ?_, ?_⟩
579	  · -- Injectivity of `φ` is exactly linear independence of `v`.
580	    rw [← LinearMap.ker_eq_bot, LinearMap.ker_eq_bot']
581	    intro g hg
582	    rw [Fintype.linearCombination_apply] at hg
583	    funext i
584	    exact Fintype.linearIndependent_iff.mp hLIv g hg i
585	  · -- The cokernel `T = N ⧸ range φ` is torsion: every element is killed by a
586	    -- non-zero divisor of `P`.
587	    intro x
588	    obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := Submodule.Quotient.mk_surjective _ x
589	    -- The images `ℓ (v i)` span `NK` over `K` (they are unit multiples of a basis).
590	    have hspan : Submodule.span K (Set.range (fun i => ℓ (v i))) = ⊤ := by
591	      refine top_unique ?_
592	      rw [← b.span_eq, Submodule.span_le]
593	      rintro _ ⟨i, rfl⟩
594	      have hbi : b i = (↑(u i) : K)⁻¹ • ℓ (v i) := by
595	        rw [hlv i, smul_smul, inv_mul_cancel₀ (u i).ne_zero, one_smul]
596	      rw [hbi]
597	      exact Submodule.smul_mem _ _ (Submodule.subset_span ⟨i, rfl⟩)
598	    -- Hence `ℓ n` is a `K`-linear combination of the `ℓ (v i)`.
599	    obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := (Submodule.mem_span_range_iff_exists_fun K).mp
600	      (show ℓ n ∈ Submodule.span K (Set.range fun i => ℓ (v i)) by
601	        rw [hspan]; exact Submodule.mem_top)
602	    -- Clear the denominators of the finitely many coefficients `c i`.
603	    obtain ⟨s, hs⟩ := IsLocalization.exist_integer_multiples S Finset.univ c
604	    have ha : ∀ i, ∃ y : P, algebraMap P K y = (s : P) • c i := fun i =>
605	      hs i (Finset.mem_univ i)
606	    choose a hae using ha
607	    -- After multiplying by `s`, `ℓ (s • n)` becomes `ℓ (φ a)`.
608	    have key : ℓ ((s : P) • n) = ℓ (Fintype.linearCombination P v a) := by
609	      rw [map_smul, ← hc, Fintype.linearCombination_apply, map_sum, Finset.smul_sum]
610	      refine Finset.sum_congr rfl (fun i _ => ?_)
611	      rw [map_smul]
612	      -- `(s:P) • (c i • ℓ v_i) = a i • ℓ v_i`
613	      rw [← algebraMap_smul K ((s : P)) (c i • ℓ (v i)), smul_smul, ← Algebra.smul_def,
614	        ← hae i, algebraMap_smul]
615	    -- So `s • n - φ a` lies in the kernel of `ℓ`, hence is killed by some `t ∈ S`.
616	    have hker : ℓ ((s : P) • n - Fintype.linearCombination P v a) = 0 := by
617	      rw [map_sub, key, sub_self]
618	    obtain ⟨t, ht⟩ := (IsLocalizedModule.eq_zero_iff S ℓ).mp hker
619	    -- The non-zero divisor `t * s` annihilates the class of `n` in `T`.
620	    refine ⟨t * s, ?_⟩
621	    rw [smul_sub, sub_eq_zero] at ht
622	    have ht2 : (↑t : P) • ((↑s : P) • n) = (↑t : P) • Fintype.linearCombination P v a := ht
623	    have hmem : (↑(t * s) : P) • n ∈ LinearMap.range (Fintype.linearCombination P v) := by
624	      rw [Submonoid.coe_mul, mul_smul, ht2, ← map_smul]
625	      exact LinearMap.mem_range_self _ _
626	    -- Conclude `(t * s) • ⟦n⟧ = 0`.
627	    rw [← Submodule.Quotient.mk_smul, Submodule.Quotient.mk_eq_zero]
628	    exact hmem

637	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
638	    (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]

648	/-! ### L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables (`lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar`)
649	
650	The single-variable elimination of the support-dimension drop needs a triangular
651	change of variables `e` making a non-zero `F ∈ A[X_0,…,X_n]` monic in `X_0` up to a
652	unit after inverting one `g ∈ A`. Mathlib's Noether-normalisation development
653	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.NoetherNormalization`) constructs exactly this transformation
654	`T` and proves the leading coefficient is a unit — but only over a *field*, and as
655	`private` lemmas. The block below replays that construction over a noetherian *domain*
656	(the field is used in Mathlib only to conclude the top coefficient is a unit; over a
657	domain it is merely non-zero, and becomes a unit after inverting it). The degree
658	bookkeeping (`degreeOf_zero_t`, `leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t`, `T_leadingcoeff_eq`) is
659	a domain-adapted transcription of the Mathlib originals. -/
660	
661	section NagataNormalization
662	
663	-- `AlgebraicGeometry.Polynomial` exists and would shadow the root namespace, so the
664	-- `Polynomial`/`MvPolynomial` lemma APIs are opened with explicit `_root_.` prefixes.
665	open _root_.Polynomial _root_.MvPolynomial _root_.Ideal Nat _root_.RingHom List
666	
667	/-- `finSuccEquiv` commutes with coefficient base change `MvPolynomial.map phi`. -/

16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45

504	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
505	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
506	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
507	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
508	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
509	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
510	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
511	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
512	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
513	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
514	
515	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
516	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
517	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
518	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
519	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
520	  image of \(\varphi\).
521	\end{proof}

580	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.

583	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of

833	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
834	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there

837	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
838	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
839	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
840	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
841	\end{lemma}

843	\begin{proof}
844	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
845	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
846	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
847	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
848	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
849	  is \(X_0\), the one eliminated by \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\); cf.\ the variable-convention
850	  note on \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
851	
852	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
853	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
854	
855	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
856	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
857	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
858	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
859	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
860	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
861	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
862	
863	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
864	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
865	  \(e(F)\) (whose \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\)-leading coefficient is a unit): the quotient
866	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
867	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
868	  \(A_g\).
869	
870	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
871	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
872	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
873	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
874	\end{proof}

913	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,

916	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
917	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE (iter-007): the induction must generalise the base domain A
918	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
919	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
920	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
921	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)

923	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
924	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
925	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
926	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
927	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
928	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
929	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
930	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
931	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
932	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
933	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
934	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
935	  corresponding polynomial ring.
936	
937	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
938	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
939	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
940	
941	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
942	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
943	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
944	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
945	  \begin{enumerate}
946	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
947	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
948	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
949	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
950	      \(P_d\)-modules
951	      \[
952	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
953	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
954	      \]
955	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
956	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
957	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
958	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
959	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
960	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
961	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
962	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
963	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
964	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
965	      to \(A_g\) (see the structure note above).
966	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Writing \(h = a / g^k\) with
967	      \(a \in A\), the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is isomorphic to \(A_{g a}\) and
968	      \((T_g)_h\) to \(T_{g a}\); thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over \((A_g)_h\) transports
969	      to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the localisation-tower free-transport
970	      fact \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}. Set \(f := g a \ne 0\); then
971	      \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
972	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
973	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
974	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite

979	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)

1080	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1081	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1082	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1083	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.

1087	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
1088	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
1089	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
1090	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
1091	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
1092	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type

1096	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1097	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1098	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1099	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1100	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1101	% by restricting to a non-empty
1102	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1103	\begin{proof}
1104	  
1105	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1106	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian; existence of such a \(U_0\)
1107	  by \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset} applied at a point of the non-empty
1108	  \(S\)). We assemble the witness \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1109	
1110	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1111	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1112	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1113	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1114	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1117	  of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the
1118	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and
1119	  finite type of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}) makes \(M_j\) a finite
1120	  \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is the data fed
1121	  to the algebraic form.
1122	
1123	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1124	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1125	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1126	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1127	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty (basic) open; over \(V\) every
1128	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) (freeness is preserved by the further localization
1129	  \(A_{f_j} \to A_f\)).
1130	
1131	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness \(\Rightarrow\) flatness, checked locally.} On each patch the
1132	  localized section module is free, hence flat over \(A_f\) by
1133	  \texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}; the fibrewise flatness conclusion (flatness of
1134	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) over \(\Gamma(S, U)\) for affine \(U \le V\), \(W \le p^{-1}U\)) then
1135	  follows from these local freeness statements by the localize-at-maximal-ideals
1136	  flatness criterion \texttt{Module.flat\_of\_isLocalized\_maximal}. This produces the
1137	  required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1138	\end{proof}
